chunk_summary: This section outlines acute malnutrition thresholds and triage decisions for children aged 6–59 months, emphasizing WHO and Indian protocols for SAM and MAM classification, clinical risk prioritization, and management strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the WHO and Indian criteria for classifying SAM and MAM in children aged 6–59 months.
Learn the importance of using both MUAC and WHZ for malnutrition assessment and their clinical implications.
Identify clinical signs and complications that necessitate urgent facility-based care for children with SAM.
Differentiate management strategies for children with and without complications of acute malnutrition.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined as nutritional edema and/or WLZ/WHZ < -3 and/or MUAC <115 mm.
Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is defined as WLZ/WHZ between -3 and < -2 and/or MUAC 115 to <125 mm, without nutritional edema.
Bilateral pitting edema in under-5 children is a diagnostic criterion for SAM until proven otherwise.
MUAC and WHZ identify overlapping but distinct high-risk groups; both should be used when feasible.
Children with SAM and complications (e.g., lethargy, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, dehydration) require urgent facility-based care, often in an NRC.
Infants under 6 months with suspected severe wasting should always be referred for facility assessment due to unique feeding and medical risks.
reference_quote: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined as nutritional edema and/or weight-for-length/height z-score (WLZ/WHZ) < -3 and/or MUAC <115 mm; MAM is WLZ/WHZ between -3 and < -2 and/or MUAC 115 to <125 mm, with no nutritional edema.
document_key_topics: Acute malnutrition thresholds for SAM and MAM
Clinical risk prioritization in malnutrition management
Triage and referral decisions for children with SAM
Use of MUAC and WHZ in malnutrition assessment
